does: This paper introduces FinRED, a specialized red-teaming framework for evaluating financial language models' safety and robustness. The framework implements a two-level taxonomy that maps international financial standards (FATF, EU DORA) to specific threat categories ranging from regulatory evasion to fraud facilitation. The core technical contribution involves a scalable pipeline that transforms real financial documents into context-rich adversarial prompts called Behavioral Prompts through an expert-defined schema. The work includes expert-validated seed generation ensuring realism and plausibility, plus a finance-specific evaluation rubric that substantially outperforms generic rubrics, reducing critical false negatives from 28 to 12. The framework bridges the gap between general-purpose LLM safety benchmarks and domain-specific financial risks that standard evaluations overlook.
connects: This work extends the broader red-teaming and adversarial evaluation literature for large language models by applying domain expertise to a previously underexplored sector. It builds on established red-teaming methodologies while responding to recognized limitations of general safety benchmarks that miss industry-specific vulnerabilities and regulatory requirements. The paper integrates frameworks from financial regulation and risk management, connecting to compliance-focused ML research. It draws inspiration from existing efforts to ground adversarial evaluation in realistic scenarios and expert feedback, while extending these approaches specifically to financial use cases where regulatory compliance, fraud prevention, and systemic trust are critical concerns that differ substantially from typical LLM safety priorities.
enables: This framework enables future research into more targeted domain-specific safety evaluation across regulated industries beyond finance. FinRED's two-level taxonomy and expert-validation methodology provide a template for constructing red-teaming benchmarks in healthcare, legal services, or other compliance-heavy sectors. The work opens directions for developing specialized safety rubrics aligned with domain-specific human judgments rather than generic criteria. It supports development of financial LLMs that can demonstrate compliance and trustworthiness to regulators and institutions, potentially facilitating responsible deployment in high-stakes financial contexts. The approach also enables investigation of how adversarial training on domain-specific threats might improve LLM safety, and how international standards can systematically ground AI safety evaluation in practical regulatory requirements.
